coredata.dc:title: Construction of up-converting fluorescent carbon quantum dots/Bi20TiO32 composites with enhanced photocatalytic properties under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Ruyi
Zhang, Linping
Xu, Hong
Zhong, Yi
Sui, Xiaofeng
Mao, Zhiping
coredata.dc:description:
To achieve the efficient utilization of solar light energy, novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts were prepared with different CQDs contents. The obtained photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, XPS, SEM, HRTEM, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The results showed that the CQDs were uniformly deposited on the surface of Bi20TiO32. The as-prepared CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composites were capable of utilizing both the visible and near-infrared light due to the active role of the carbon nanodots as up-converting photo-sensitizers. By the LC–MS-MS analysis, the herbicide isoproturon molecular could be firstly degraded to several intermediates and then completely mineralized. The CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts with a CQDs content of 1% exhibited the optimum photocatalytic activity and could degrade 98.1% of isoproturon within 48h. Its degradation rate constant was approximately 4.3 times higher than that of pure Bi20TiO32. The main active species in the photo-degradation process was h+ and
O2 −. To further study the recycle ability of CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst, the PAN supported CQDs/Bi20TiO32 nanofiber mat was prepared by coaxial electrospinning method. The composite nanofiber materials showed good photocatalytic activity and recyclability, which had potential applications in water/air purification field and biochemical protective clothing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots
Bi20TiO32
Isoproturon degradation
Nanofiber mat

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL Emission Spectra and XRD patterns
Photocatalytic activity of PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun nanofiber mats

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Technology R & D Program
National High-tech Research and Development Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National High-tech Research and Development Program

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars , State Education Ministry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper is dedicated to Prof. Thomas C. W. Mak on the occasion of his 80th birthday. This work was financially supported by the National Key Technology R & D Program (No. 2014BAC13B02 ), the National High-tech Research and Development Program (863 Program No. 2013AA06A307), the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars , State Education Ministry (No. 13W10539 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Down-converted and (b) up-converted photoluminescence spectra of CQDs aqueous solution under different excitation wavelengths. Inset of (a): images of CQDs in aqueous solution illuminated under white light (left) and UV light (right).
Photoluminescence spectra of CQDs aqueous solution and deionized water under the excitation wavelength of 360nm.
XRD patterns of CQDs, Bi20TiO32, and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32.
FT-IR patterns of CQDs, Bi20TiO32, and CQDs/Bi20TiO32 with different contents of CQDs.
XPS spectra of Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts: (a) survey of the sample, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) Ti 2p, and (e) C 1s.
(a) Transient photocurrent response and (b) electrochemical impedance spectra of Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of the isoproturon in the presence of Bi20TiO32 and CQDs/Bi20TiO32 with different contents of CQDs under visible light irradiation, (b) kinetic fit for the degradation of the isoproturon with Bi20TiO32 and CQDs/Bi20TiO32 with different contents of CQDs.
Comparison of photocatalytic activities of 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 for the degradation of isoproturon with or without t-BuOH, AO and BQ under visible light irradiation.
Plausible photocatalytic degradation pathway of isoproturon.
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic mechanism for CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalyst under solar light irradiation.

SEM images of (a) pure PAN and (b) PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fiber mats, (c) photocatalytic degradation of isoproturon in the presence of pure PAN electrospun fiber mats and PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fiber mats under the visible light irradiation, (d) recycling test of PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fiber mats towards isoproturon degradation.
Isoproturon and intermediates identified by LC–MS-MS.

head.ce:title: Construction of up-converting fluorescent carbon quantum dots/Bi20TiO32 composites with enhanced photocatalytic properties under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Science and Technology of Eco-textile, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, No. 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Science and Technology of Eco-textile
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hierarchical carbon quantum dots/Bi20TiO32 with enhanced photoactivity towards the degradation of isoproturon.

To achieve the efficient utilization of solar light energy, novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts were prepared with different CQDs contents. The obtained photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, XPS, SEM, HRTEM, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The results showed that the CQDs were uniformly deposited on the surface of Bi20TiO32. The as-prepared CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composites were capable of utilizing both the visible and near-infrared light due to the active role of the carbon nanodots as up-converting photo-sensitizers. By the LC–MS-MS analysis, the herbicide isoproturon molecular could be firstly degraded to several intermediates and then completely mineralized. The CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts with a CQDs content of 1% exhibited the optimum photocatalytic activity and could degrade 98.1% of isoproturon within 48h. Its degradation rate constant was approximately 4.3 times higher than that of pure Bi20TiO32. The main active species in the photo-degradation process was h+ and
O2 −. To further study the recycle ability of CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst, the PAN supported CQDs/Bi20TiO32 nanofiber mat was prepared by coaxial electrospinning method. The composite nanofiber materials showed good photocatalytic activity and recyclability, which had potential applications in water/air purification field and biochemical protective clothing.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysts are used in many fields, such as photolysis of water to H2 and O2 [1–3], photo-decomposition of toxic chemicals [4–6] and photo-reduction of CO2 to renewable fuels [7–9]. Nevertheless, the most widely used photocatalyst TiO2 can only absorb short wavelength light in the UV region, which accounts for just less than 5% of the total solar energy [10–12]. To broaden the use of abundant sunlight, great efforts have been put on the development of highly efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts. Among the common semiconductors, bismuth titanates as visible light responsive photocatalysts have aroused a great deal of research interests for sake of their relatively narrow band gap energy (2.3–2.8eV) [13–16].

Bi20TiO32 is a desired visible light responsive photocatalyst amongst various bismuth titanates. Due to the hybridized Bi 6s and O 2p valence band (VB), the band gap energy of Bi20TiO32 is only 2.36eV, resulting in the high photocatalytic activity [17–19]. The Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst shows good capability of degrading organic pollutants, such as pyridine, phenol and dyes [5,20]. But its photocatalytic activity is still limited by the slow rate of charge transfer and the fast recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Currently, the main strategy to further improve photocatalytic activity of Bi20TiO32 is to construct the Z-scheme heterojunctions structure with Ag-AgX [21,22]. However, two main problems existing in this strategy are the usage of noble metals and to find two photocatalysts with matched band structures. Therefore, the metal-free materials with high electron conductivity and solar light absorption ability are highly desirable for modification of semiconductor photocatalysts.

As a novel class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit many fascinating physical and chemical properties, such as high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and luminescence property [23,24]. They have been widely employed in sensors [25], photovoltaic devices [26], bioimaging [27], etc. Notably, CQDs also possess unique optical and electronic properties including high stability against photo-bleaching, excellent electron transfer ability and up-converting photoluminescence. These properties make CQDs a promising candidate, being capable of both broadening the utilization range of solar light and promoting the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Recently, they have been introduced into the photocatalysis filed to improve the photocatalytic activities of some semiconductors, such as TiO2, Fe3O4, Cu2O, Bi2WO6 and BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I) [28–34]. The band gap (2.36eV) of Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst corresponds to the absorption edge of visible light (525nm). The up-converting photoluminescence of CQDs means that they can be excited by near-infrared (NIR) light (750–1000nm) and emit low-wavelength light (<600nm). Based on the above discussion, the combined photocatalysis system of CQDs and Bi20TiO32 could take full advantage of the sunlight spectrum. To the best of our knowledge, no study on CQDs modified Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation against organic pollutants has been reported so far.

Herein, the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalyst was successfully prepared by anchoring novel up-converting CQDs on the surface of Bi20TiO32 nanowires via solvothermal synthesis and oil bath reflux method. The phase structures, morphologies, optical and photo-electrochemical properties were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic property was evaluated by degradation of the colorless herbicide isoproturon under visible light irradiation. In this system, CQDs not only facilitate the separation of photo-generated carriers but also widen the absorption range of solar light. The possible photocatalytic mechanism was put forward in view of the free radicals trapping experiments and intermediate products analysis. To further study the recycle ability of the photocatalyst, polyacrylonitrile (PAN) supported CQDs/Bi20TiO32 core-shell fiber mat was constructed by coaxial electrospinning technology. The obtained composite fiber mats showed good photocatalytic property and recyclability.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), benzyl alcohol (C7H8O), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), ethylenediamine (H2NCH2CH2NH2), absolute ethanol (CH3CH2OH), tert-Butanol (t-BuOH), oxalic acid (AO) and Benzoquinone (BQ) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tetraisopropyl orthotitanate (Ti(OC3H7)4) was obtained from the Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. N,N-Dimethylacetamide (DMAc) was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All of the above chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Isoproturon (>99.6% pure, HPLC grade) was purchased from the AccuStandard, USA. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) with a molecular weight of 60,000 was supplied by Zhejiang Shangyu Wuyue Economic and Trade Co., Ltd.

Pure Bi20TiO32 was prepared via a solvothermal process followed by post-heat-treatment route according to the previous report [21]. The CQDs were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method [35]. In a typical preparation of CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite, a certain amount of CQDs was dissolved in 50mL deionized water. Then 0.4g of Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts was added into the above solution and ultrasonic treated for 30min at room temperature. The suspension was refluxed in an oil bath at 90°C for 5h. The product was filtered and dried at 70°C for 12h. CQDs/Bi20TiO32 samples with different contents of CQDs (0.5%, 1%, 3%, and 5%) were prepared via a similar route and labeled 0.5%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, 3%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, and 5%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, respectively.

The PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 nanofibers were fabricated by coaxial electrospinning. The sheath solution was prepared with PAN, DMAc and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32. First, 1.0g of PAN powder was added into 10mL of DMAc, the solution was vigorously stirred at 75°C for 6h. Then, 0.75g of 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite was added into the PAN solution and vigorously stirred for another 5h to form sheath solution. The core solution was prepared by dissolving 1.5g of PAN in 10mL of DMAc. Electrostatic spinning voltage was set to 20kV. The core and sheath solutions were pumped at feed rates of 0.3mL/h and 0.4mL/h, respectively. The electrospun fiber mats were collected on the aluminum foil covering on the collector at a distance of 20cm. The obtained product was heated in an oven at 100°C overnight to remove the solvent. For comparison, pure PAN nanofiber mat was also prepared by electrospinning.

The crystal structures and phase data for the photocatalysts were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ltd., Japan) with Cu Kα (λ=1.54056Å). The morphologies of the photocatalysts were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The structures and morphologies of electrospun nanofibers were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TM-1000, HITACHI, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained using a Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. Surface electronic states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα source at 1486.6eV. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the prepared samples were detected on an Edinburgh FS5 Fluorescence Spectrometer. To determine the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs, photoluminescence spectra of CQDs aqueous solution and deionized water was further measured by Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometers (QM/TM, PTI, USA). The transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured with an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 760D Chenhua Instrument Company).

The herbicide isoproturon was chosen to investigate the photocatalytic activities of different CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. A 500W Xe lamp with a 400nm cutoff filter was used as the visible light source. 50mg of each of the photocatalyst was added into 50mL of isoproturon aqueous solution (20mg/L) in a test tube. The distance between light source and test tube was approximately 5cm. Before photoreaction, the suspension was magnetically stirred for 30min in dark to reach the absorption-desorption equilibrium under normal atmospheric conditions. At given time intervals, constant amounts of the reaction solution were withdrawn and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst particulates. The filtrate of the isoproturon solution was analyzed by the Agilent-1100 high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a C-18 Phenomenex reverse phase column. The test wavelength was set to 239nm. The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and water with a volume ratio of 50:50 at a flow rate of 1mL/min, and 20μL of the sample was injected each time. The degradation intermediates of isoproturon were further analyzed by Varian LC–MS-MS. To investigate the roles of active species in the photocatalysis process, the experiments of active species (
OH, h+ and
O2 −) capture were performed through adding 1mmol/L of tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH), ammonium oxalate (AO) and benzoquinone (BQ), respectively.

The photocatalytic activity of PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 nanofiber mat was also evaluated by the photo-degradation of isoproturon under visible light irradiation. All of the photocatalysis reactions were performed in a quartz reaction cell. The round pure PAN and PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 nanofiber mats (diameter of fiber mats: 8cm, weight of fiber mats: approximately 0.1g) were put into petri dishes (diameter: 9cm, height: 1.5cm) filled with 50mL of 15mg/L isoproturon solution. The petri dishes were placed at a distance of 5cm from the visible light source. During the photocatalytic tests, fixed amount of the reaction solution was taken out at given time intervals, followed by centrifugation to remove the impurities. The concentration of the isoproturon solution was measured by HPLC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL Emission Spectra and XRD patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The unique PL property of CQDs was investigated with different excitation wavelengths. As a reference, the PL spectra of the deionized water were also detected and no obvious photoluminescence signals were found. Fig.1 (a) showed the down-converted PL spectra under the excitation wavelength ranging from 350 to 550nm. Obviously, excited by short wavelength light, the CQDs could emit long wavelength PL from 400 to 600nm. The PL emission spectra exhibited a gradual red-shift and the PL intensity decreased gradually as the excitation wavelength increased. This phenomenon could be explained by the optical selection of different surface defect states near the Fermi level of CQDs [36]. In the inset of Fig.1(a), the aqueous solution of CQDs showed light yellow under irradiation of white light and bright blue fluorescence under irradiation of UV light. Fig.1(b) showed the up-converted PL spectra using the NIR excitation light. The CQDs were excited by NIR light and emitted the up-converted PL at the visible light regions. The PL spectra also moved to longer wavelength region as the excitation light altered from 650 to 900nm. The strongest PL emission peak appeared at 525nm under the excitation wavelength 850nm. The broad PL peak was ascribed to the relaxation of electrons from a higher energy state of π orbital (LUMO) to the σ orbital [37]. The excellent PL property, especially up-converted PL, extended the utilization range from the visible light to near-infrared region.

To determine the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs, photoluminescence spectra of CQDs aqueous solution and deionized water under the excitation wavelength of 360nm was further measured and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 0.315 using the equation:
(1)
QY = (
A
u
-
A
s
) / (
B
s
-
B
u
)
where QY is the quantum yield, B is the integrated fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength, A is the integrated fluorescence intensity at the luminescence light. The subscript “s” refers to the reference and “u” refers to the sample.

The phase structures of the prepared samples were characterized by XRD analysis. The XRD patterns of CQDs in Fig. 3 showed a broad peak, which was ascribed to the intrinsic amorphous nature of CQDs [35]. All the diffraction peaks of the prepared Bi20TiO32 could be indexed to the standard XRD patterns of tetragonal Bi20TiO32 (JCPDS42-0202) with the major peaks at 2θ=28.2°, 31.62°, 32.88°, 46.26°, 54.16°, 55.77° and 57.91°, corresponding to the diffractions of the (201), (002), (220), (222), (203), (421) and (402) planes [19]. With respect to the composite CQDs/Bi20TiO32, all of the diffraction peaks were well matched to Bi20TiO32 and no obvious peaks of CQDs were observed compared with pure Bi20TiO32, which was in agreement with other similar systems [32,38]. This was probably attributed to the low content and high dispersion degree of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 materials.

The size distribution and morphology of the obtained CQDs were characterized by HRTEM. As revealed in Fig.4 (a), the well-dispersed CQDs were near spherical particles. The HRTEM of CQDs in Fig.4(b) demonstrated that the size of CQDs was approximately 5nm and the lattice spacing was around 0.21nm corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [39]. The microstructure of CQDs/Bi20TiO32 was also investigated by HETEM. It can be seen from Fig.4(c) that the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst showed nanowire structure with diameter of approximately 20nm and numerous nanodots with size of 3–5nm were uniformly distributed on the surface of the nanowire. From the high magnification image in Fig.4(d), the lattice spacing of nanodots was determined to be 0.21nm consistent with the (100) crystal plan of CQDs. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing around 0.31nm corresponded to the (201) crystal plane of Bi20TiO32 [19]. The above results confirmed that the CQDs were successfully anchored on the surface of the Bi20TiO32 nanowires.

The presence of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalysts was further validated by FT-IR analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . For pure CQDs, the characteristic absorption bands could be observed as follows: stretching vibrations of COH at 3430cm−1, CH at 2923cm−1 and 2850cm−1, asymmetric stretching vibrations of CNHC at 1126cm−1, bending vibrations of NH at 1570cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1635cm−1. The absorption peaks at 514cm−1 and 820cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of Bi20TiO32 were attributed to the stretching vibration of BiO [33] and TiO [40], respectively. For the 0.5%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalysts, their absorption spectra were similar to that of Bi20TiO32 as a result of the low content of CQDs. As the amount of CQDs increased gradually, the two characteristic absorption bands of CQDs at 1570cm−1 and 1635cm−1 appeared in the FT-IR spectra of 3%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 and 5%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32. Thus, the FT-IR results further indicated that the Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst was modified by CQDs successfully.

XPS analysis was employed to determine the surface chemical composition of pure Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32. The full survey spectra in Fig.6 (a) demonstrated the existence of Bi, O, Ti and C elements in both Bi20TiO32 and CQDs/Bi20TiO32 samples. In the Bi 4f spectra (Fig. 6(b)), the peaks centered at 158.9 and 164.2eV were assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, suggesting the Bi3+ in the Bi20TiO32 crystal structure [22]. Compared with Bi20TiO32, the Bi 4f peak in the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalyst exhibited a slight shift, indicating that the chemical environment of Bi element changed owing to the interaction between Bi and C. The O 1s peak at 530.0eV in Fig.6(c) was attributed to the oxygen in the Bi20TiO32 crystals. The two peaks at 457.9 and 466.0eV in Fig.6(d) were ascribed to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of Ti4+ in the Bi20TiO32 crystals, respectively [22]. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 was shown in Fig.6(e), which revealed the surface functional groups on the CQDs. The signals at 284.4, 285.4 and 287.8eV could be assigned to the CC bond with sp2 orbital, CO bond and CN bond, respectively [35]. Consistent with the FT-IR analysis, the XPS results confirmed the coexistence of CQDs and Bi20TiO32 in the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalyst.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra analysis were generally carried out to investigate the recombination procedure of photo-generated hole-electron pairs in photocatalysts [41,42]. Fig. 7 showed the PL spectra of pure Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 excited by 320nm. Because the PL emission originated from the energy dissipation during the recombination process of photo-generated holes and electrons, the stronger intensity of PL spectra represented the higher recombination rate of hole-electron pairs. As shown in Figs. 7 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 exhibited a lower PL emission intensity than pure Bi20TiO32. This implied that the CQDs modification could effectively inhibit the recombination of hole-electron pairs in Bi20TiO32 and improve the utilization efficiency of solar light [43].

The transient photocurrent tests were performed to study the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers of the as-prepared Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32. As presented in Fig.8 (a), the fast transient photocurrent responses through several on-off irradiation cycles were observed for both Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrodes. The 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 exhibited a higher photocurrent signal than Bi20TiO32 electrode. The obvious improvement of photocurrent response demonstrated that much more photo-generated charge carriers were produced and the electron-hole pairs could separate in the 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 more efficiently [44]. To further investigate the electron transfer properties of the Bi20TiO32 and 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (ESI) analysis of different electrodes were carried out, as shown in Fig.8(b). Distinctly, 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 showed a smaller Nyquist circle in the high frequency region in comparison with pure Bi20TiO32. The phenomenon revealed that 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 had much lower electron-transfer resistance than pure Bi20TiO32 [45]. It could be attributed to the fact that the conjugated π structure of CQDs could facilitate the transfer of photo-generated electrons from the CB of Bi20TiO32 to CQDs and resulted in an effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [46]. Both transient photocurrent tests and ESI analysis implied that the introduction of CQDs could inhibit the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic activity of Bi20TiO32.

Isoproturon, as a phenylurea herbicide, is widely applied to control weeds in winter cereals. The residues of isoproturon in soil and surface water are severely harmful to microbial communities and humans. The photocatalytic activities of the prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of the herbicide isoproturon under the visible light irradiation, as shown in Fig.9 (a). The absorption-desorption equilibrium was achieved between the photocatalysts and isoproturon in dark for 30min. The photolysis curve showed that isoproturon was hardly degraded without photocatalysts, indicating the effect of photolysis on isoproturon concentration could be neglected. The pure Bi20TiO32 showed poor photocatalytic activity and removed only 48.6% of isoproturon after 60h of irradiation. After the introduction of different contents of CQDs, 61.9%, 98.1%, 83.6%, and 72.3% of the isoproturon could be degraded by 0.5%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, 3%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32, and 5%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 over the same irradiation time, respectively. The 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity, which could degrade almost all of the isoproturon within 48h. The improvement of photocatalytic activity could be explained in two aspects. On the one hand, the perfect electron transfer property of CQDs could effectively accelerate the separation of hole-electron pairs generated in Bi20TiO32. On the other hand, CQDs with the up-converted PL property could absorb NIR light and emit visible light, which fell in the scope of responsive spectra of Bi20TiO32. Thus much more hole-electron pairs were produced to participate in the photocatalytic reaction. Nevertheless, the CQDs also absorbed visible light and too many CQDs covered on the surface of Bi20TiO32 would inevitably impede the light absorption of Bi20TiO32. Consequently, the increasing content of CQDs resulted in a decrease of the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of isoproturon by Bi20TiO32 and CQDs/Bi20TiO32 were investigated, as shown in Fig.9(b). The changes of the isoproturon concentration versus irradiation time followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics plot. Consistent with the degradation curve of isoproturon, the degradation rate constant of the 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 reached the maximum which was approximately 4.3 times higher than that of pure Bi20TiO32. The photocatalytic degradation experiment of isoproturon implied that the appropriate modification by CQDs was an effective route to improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst.

To determine the active species during the photo-degradation process, free radicals trapping experiments were carried out using different scavengers: t-BuOH for
OH, AO for h+ and BQ for
O2 − [47,48]. The effects of these scavengers on photo-degradation of the isoproturon were shown in Fig. 10 . Compared with the photo-degradation result without scavengers, the addition of t-BuOH had no obvious influence on the photocatalytic reaction, indicating that
OH was not the main active species in this system. But the photocatalytic activity was significantly restrained when AO and BQ were introduced into the reaction system. The effect of BQ on the photo-degradation rate was more obvious than that of AO. Therefore, it inferred that h+ and
O2 − were the main active species in this photocatalysis system and
O2 − played a comparatively major role in photo-degradation of isoproturon.

The intermediate products were detected and identified by LC–MS-MS. The data in Table 1 showed that a total of seven intermediates were found during isoproturon degradation. Similar intermediates were also detected by other authors [49]. Based on the results, a plausible photocatalytic degradation pathway of the isoproturon was put forward in Fig. 11 . The molecular structure of intermediates indicated that isoproturon was attacked by h+ and
O2 − in three main groups: (a) isopropyl chain, (b) imino group linked to benzenering, (c) dimethylamino group. The attack on the isopropyl chain and dimethylamino group led to the formation of compound (m/z 149). The intermediate product (m/z 136) can be considered as a result of the consecutive attacks on dimethylamino group and carbanylgroup. In turn, the product (m/z 136) could suffer demethylation on isopropyl chain, resulting in aniline (m/z 94). The amino group on aniline could be further oxidized to nitro group, leading to the product (m/z 124). In addition, the isoproturon could be also attacked on the imino group, leaving the formation of isopropylbenzene (m/z 121). The consecutive demethylation of isopropyl chain on isopropylbenzene resulted in the intermediate products (m/z 107 and 93). According to the previous reports [49], it can be inferred that the benzenering of these intermediates could be further attacked by the active species and successive oxidations led to aldehydes, ketones and organic acids. Finally, these small molecules could be completely mineralized.

Based on the free radicals trapping experiments and intermediate products analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/Bi20TiO32 was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 12 . Under the solar light irradiation, Bi20TiO32 was excited by the visible light to produce electrons on the CB and holes on the VB, respectively. Due to the delocalized conjugated π structure of CQDs [50], photo-generated electrons could easily transfer to CQDs, accelerating the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs [51–53]. The electrons on CQDs reacted with O2 in aqueous solution to yield
O2 −. A large amount of
O2 − and photo-generated holes with oxidation capability could attack different functional groups of isoproturon to produce several intermediates. These intermediates were further degraded by
O2 − and photo-generated holes to CO2, H2O and HNO3. Without CQDs, most of the photo-generated electrons and holes would quickly recombine with each other rather than participate in the photocatalytic reaction. Besides, the CQDs with up-converted PL property could absorb the NIR light and emit visible light, which in turn excited Bi20TiO32 to form much more electron-hole pairs. It meant that the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 could utilize much wider range of solar light to improve the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, it was reported that CQDs possessed good adsorptive performance toward organic pollutants, which was favorable for the photocatalytic degradation of the isoproturon [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun nanofiber mats
ce:section.ce:para: The immobilization of nanostructured photocatalysts was beneficial for their separation and recovery process. Electrospinning technology was a facile approach to obtain nanofibers. The large surface area and abundant pore structure were beneficial to improve the contact probability between photocatalysts and pollutants [54]. Due to their good electrospinnability and fine stability [55], PAN electrospun nanofibers were usually chosen as the supporters for photocatalysts. Thus, the loaded photocatalysts could be recycled easily, which would avoid their loss in application. Moreover, the hydrophobic property and low density of PAN made it easy for electrospun nanofibers to float on the liquid, which would optimize the efficiency of light irradiation by avoiding the hindrance of light source penetration [54,56]. Herein, we explored the possibility of immobilizing 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts on the PAN nanofibers through the coaxial electrospinning method. From the SEM image shown in Fig.13 (b), the obtained PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fibers exhibited uniform diameters in the range of 500–700nm. Compared with the pure PAN nanofibers, the surfaces of PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fibers were decorated with large amounts of photocatalysts. The photocatalytic activity of electrospun fiber mats was also studied by the degradation of the isoproturon. As shown in Fig.13(c), the pure PAN electrospun fiber mats could absorb approximately 36.9% of isoproturon in aqueous solution after 72h. For PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fiber mats, CQDs/Bi20TiO32 endowed the PAN nanofiber with an obvious photocatalytic activity, and approximately 82.3% of isoproturon was degraded under the visible light irradiation for 72h. To verify the recycling ability of the composite materials, the cyclic degradation experiments of the isoproturon were carried out three times under the visible light irradiation. It was shown in Fig.13(d) that PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 electrospun fiber mat could still degrade 66.5% of isoproturon after three cycles. The slight decline of photocatalytic activity was attributed to the inevitable photo-corrosion of photocatalysts after such a long time irradiation. Based on the above discussion, the 1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts could be well immobilized on the PAN nanofibers by electrospinning technology. The obtained composite electrospun fiber mats exhibited good photocatalytic activity and recycling stability for the degradation of the isoproturon. The composite materials would have potential applications in biochemical protection and water/air purification field.

In summary, CQDs embedded wire-like Bi20TiO32 composites were successfully synthesized as efficient photocatalysts. The photocatalytic activity was investigated by the degradation of the herbicide isoproturon under visible light irradiation. Compared with pure Bi20TiO32, the CQDs/Bi20TiO32 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity. The optimized composite (1%-CQDs/Bi20TiO32) showed the highest visible light induced photocatalytic activity towards the isoproturon degradation (up to 98% degradation efficiency within 48h). The up-conversion property and electron acceptor property of CQDs facilitated the usage of solar light and blocked the electron/hole recombination process, respectively. The synergistic effects of optical properties of CQDs and Bi20TiO32 produced a remarkable visible light responsive photocatalyst. By the LC–MS-MS analysis, the isoproturon molecular could be firstly degraded to several intermediates and then completely mineralized. Based on the free radicals trapping experiments and intermediate products analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/Bi20TiO32 for isoproturon degradation was proposed. Moreover, the PAN-CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite nanofiber prepared through coaxial electrospinning method demonstrated good photocatalytic performance and recycling stability. The CQDs/Bi20TiO32 composite has great potential as an environmentally friendly photocatalyst with wider utilization of the whole solar spectrum including the near-infrared (NIR) range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper is dedicated to Prof. Thomas C. W. Mak on the occasion of his 80th birthday. This work was financially supported by the National Key Technology R & D Program (No. 2014BAC13B02), the National High-tech Research and Development Program (863 Program No. 2013AA06A307), the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, State Education Ministry (No. 13W10539).
